title: El Niño could drag down the global economy by almost $700 billion or $3 trillion—and the choice is ours
gist: A PIIE analysis by Cullen S. Hendrix estimates the 2026 Super El Niño will cut global GDP by about $686 billion in year one and $3.1 trillion over five years, with losses concentrated in tropical LMICs like Ecuador, Peru, and Indonesia and compounded by the Strait of Hormuz closure and cuts to WFP funding. Most of the five-year damage is described as still preventable through pre-positioned IMF/World Bank credit facilities and food aid, making the next few months a critical policy window.

insights.claim: A Super El Niño arriving in 2026, if comparable in intensity to the 1997-98 event, is estimated to cut global GDP by about $686 billion in the first year and by roughly $3.1 trillion cumulatively over five years due to compounding lost investment.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Treat 2026-27 as a global growth headwind of roughly 0.6% of GDP in year one, with material upside risk to the loss estimate if the event proves stronger than 1997-98.
insights.quote: the contemporaneous economic losses will be on the order of $686 billion. Unless the international community and national-level policymakers start acting now, those losses could rise to $3.1 trillion over the next five years.
insights.locator: The National Weather Service announcement of June 11, 2026

insights.claim: The cost estimates are anchored in Christopher Callahan and Justin Mankin's 2023 study, which introduces 'teleconnection strength'—how strongly a country's local temperature and rainfall respond to eastern Pacific warming—as the metric for scaling El Niño GDP impacts across roughly 88 exposed countries with a collective GDP base of about $38 trillion.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Teleconnection strength gives a country-selection lens for climate-driven GDP risk—screen exposure by this metric rather than by geography or income level alone.
insights.quote: essentially, how strongly its local temperature and rainfall respond to warming in the eastern Pacific.

insights.claim: Five-year cumulative El Niño losses are estimated at about 4.5 times the year-one loss, meaning roughly $2.4 trillion of the projected $3.1 trillion five-year toll comes from compounding investment losses in years two through five rather than from the initial shock itself.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: The bulk of the projected damage ($2.4 trillion) is not yet locked in—it hinges on policy response over the next few years, making this a monitorable, actionable window rather than a fixed loss.
insights.quote: approximately 4.5 times, derived from their figure 1D—yields a five-year estimate of roughly $3.1 trillion

insights.claim: The ten most strongly teleconnected countries to El Niño are Ecuador, Peru, Indonesia, Malaysia, Suriname, Panama, Nicaragua, Togo, Zambia, and Costa Rica—all lower- or middle-income tropical economies.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: These economies and their key commodities and currencies are the highest-beta plays on El Niño-driven weather disruption and warrant close monitoring through late 2026.
insights.locator: Effects fall heavily on the tropics

insights.claim: Of the $686 billion in estimated year-one global losses, $535 billion (about 78%) is concentrated in just 51 countries with a combined GDP of roughly $16 trillion, implying a 3.3% GDP hit for that group versus a 0.6% global average.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Global GDP-weighted averages mask a regional shock roughly five times sharper, concentrated in a specific basket of LMICs—relevant for sovereign risk and commodity-supply exposure in that group.
insights.quote: Of the estimated $686 billion total loss, $535 billion is concentrated in 51 countries with a combined GDP of approximately $16 trillion; for those countries, the losses amount to 3.3 percent of GDP.
insights.locator: Effects fall heavily on the tropics

themes.theme: How El Niño destroys growth: a reinforcing channel chain

insights.claim: El Niño's growth damage compounds through multiple simultaneous channels: failed harvests raise food import bills and drain foreign exchange, flooding destroys roads and power grids and crowds out public investment during rebuilding, and elevated disease burden cuts labor productivity while diverting household and government spending toward treatment instead of investment.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Model El Niño impact as a multiplicative shock across trade balances, infrastructure capex, and health spending simultaneously, not as a single-channel commodity story.
insights.quote: Failed harvests reduce farm incomes and drive up food import bills, diverting foreign exchange from productive investment.
insights.locator: The channels by which El Niños suppress growth

insights.claim: Many of the most El Niño-exposed countries already carry heavy debt loads from the COVID-19 pandemic, leaving them limited fiscal space for countercyclical response, and the resulting hardship is said to significantly raise the risk of civil conflict—creating a second-order growth drag beyond the direct weather damage.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Sovereign debt levels and conflict risk in high-teleconnection LMICs should be watched as leading indicators of El Niño spillover beyond the direct agriculture channel.
insights.quote: These various sources of hardship increase the risk of civil conflict significantly, with further growth-destroying effects.
insights.locator: The channels by which El Niños suppress growth

themes.theme: Colliding shocks: Hormuz closure meets El Niño

insights.claim: The roughly four-month closure of the Strait of Hormuz is simultaneously working through the global economy—spurring inflation and limiting fertilizer access—and is expected to combine with El Niño-driven harvest failures to cause a food price spike in late 2026 to early 2027.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Watch for a compounding food-price spike (agriculture, fertilizer, shipping) in Q4 2026-Q1 2027 driven by two independent shocks stacking—a distinct catalyst separate from either shock alone.
insights.quote: the effects of the roughly four-month closure of the Strait of Hormuz are working their way through the global economy, spurring inflation and limiting access to fertilizers and likely causing a food price spike in late 2026 to early 2027
insights.locator: The channels by which El Niños suppress growth

insights.claim: The loss estimates assume an E-index value of 3.5 for the 2026 El Niño versus the actual 3.84 recorded in 1997-98, and the author notes evidence suggests this year's event could surpass 1997-98 in strength, meaning the $686 billion and $3.1 trillion figures should be read as a conservative floor rather than a worst case.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Treat the headline loss estimates as a downside floor; confirmation of an E-index above 3.84 would imply both the $686 billion and $3.1 trillion figures should be revised upward.
insights.quote: Evidence is beginning to suggest this year's El Niño could surpass 1997–98, so the estimates presented here can be considered conservative.

themes.theme: Policy window and geopolitical headwinds to relief funding

insights.claim: Multilateral development banks—the World Bank, regional development banks, and the IMF—should pre-position concessional credit facilities for high-teleconnection LMICs before the El Niño peak, because affected countries historically face widening sovereign bond spreads exactly when they most need to borrow.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: MDB/IMF facility announcements in H2 2026 are a signal of whether the $2.4 trillion in avoidable losses will actually be mitigated; sovereign spread widening in high-teleconnection LMICs is an early-warning indicator to track.

insights.claim: The IMF's existing Resilience and Sustainability Facility is described as too narrow and undercapitalized for rapid climate-shock response, and the author calls for its eligibility requirements to be waived or streamlined now rather than after the El Niño peak.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: A near-term reform or expansion of the IMF's Resilience and Sustainability Facility would be a concrete signal that the policy window is being used rather than missed.
insights.quote: its eligibility requirements and capitalization make it a niche instrument rather than a rapid-response one

insights.claim: The World Food Program's capacity to pre-position food aid ahead of the 2026 El Niño is constrained because the United States cut its WFP contributions by 54% in 2025, while the Trump administration—with tailwinds from Russian president Vladimir Putin—has been the driving force behind Europe's rearmament push that is diverting donor resources away from humanitarian funding.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Reduced WFP funding capacity right as El Niño and Hormuz-driven food price spikes converge raises the probability of humanitarian shortfalls and associated instability in exposed LMICs going unaddressed in late 2026-2027.
insights.quote: the United States has been pivotal in both, slashing its contributions to the WFP by 54 percent in 2025, and the Trump administration has been—with strong tailwinds courtesy of Russian president Vladimir Putin—the driving force behind Europe's rearmament push.
insights.locator: Global food reserves and humanitarian pre-positioning
